section_title: W.E.N.T. SERIES 5 OVERVIEW
content:
This is the final installment for the 'What Every New and/or Aspiring Forex Trader Wants to Know' review.
This material has condensed a lot of content from the last four years, and while it's not intended to replace
going through all the old material, it's a means to get you back up to speed with the core essential tenets of
what I use for my trading analysis concepts and how I arrive at market ideas and my framework.

section_title: ABBREVIATED STUDY AND TOOL SELECTION
content:
It's important to understand this is an abbreviated form of study. I've left out some components, like Trader's
Trinity, the overall pattern, and Stinger patterns, because the majority of our Inner Circle Trader community
members have been exposed to a lot of information. With twenty years of trading, I have many tools, but
not every tool is applicable to every trader. I've become more cognizant of this due to feedback,
acknowledging that I tend to share everything, sometimes too fast, even though it takes a lot of time to
digest.

section_title: SUPPORT, RESISTANCE, AND MACRO TRENDS
content:
It's foolish to think that watching a video will instantly make you a trading 'maven.' You need tools and
applications that resonate with your persona and psyche as a trader. I went back to the core roots of all my
success, which are simply support and resistance, and understanding macro fundamental-driven trends.
These are higher time frame based only
- monthly, weekly, daily. Having this as your foundation makes
shorter term time frame analysis concepts much more accurate and sensitive to your needs as a trader.

section_title: DIRECTIONAL PREMISE
content:
- THE MOST ASKED QUESTION
In previous sessions, we extensively covered directional premise, as it's the most common question I
receive: 'How do I know which direction the market is going today?' While I'm not one hundred percent
accurate every day, my tools guide me to be in the upper ninety percent bracket for directional premise.
There's no mystery; it's simply due to discipline and trusting the tools to adhere to a certain frame of mind
when studying price.

section_title: HIGHER TIME FRAME TRENDS AND MARKET TIDES
content:
Higher time frame trends tend to stay in one direction for a long period. There might be minor corrections or
pauses, but generally, the macro major trends, or 'tides,' keep moving in the same direction until they meet
greater or equal resistance, or significant political/economic changes occur. The concepts in this final
session will combine previous material with additional paramount points for the execution stage.

section_title: INTRODUCING RISK ON / RISK OFF SCENARIOS
content:
First, we'll talk about Risk On and Risk Off. In a Risk On environment, you typically see equities, stocks,
commodities, and higher yielding currencies rally or move higher in price. The dollar generally moves down.
I dislike saying it, but the dollar, having been 'beaten up' over the last couple of decades and falling out of
favor globally, still remains the reserve currency as of 2014.

section_title: UNDERSTANDING RISK OFF AND MARKET IMPACT
content:
If we expect firmer prices in the dollar, that equates to a Risk Off scenario. This translates to lower equity or
stock prices, lower higher-yielding foreign currencies, and dropping commodity prices. You don't need to
track every single commodity, stock, or index; just a few will give you a clearer daily depiction, especially
with the accompaniment of the US dollar.

section_title: THE US DOLLAR AS A MARKET BAROMETER
content:
What we're showing here is a US dollar daily chart. If you're familiar with my market reviews or
commentary, I generally discuss the dollar first because it's my barometer. It tells me if we're in a trending,
consolidation, or risk-on/risk-off environment. A lot of information is derived from simply understanding the
dollar, especially since I trade the majors in Forex.

section_title: PARAMOUNT MACRO VIEW FOR LONGEVITY
content:
Understanding the dollar is paramount for knowing where price is most likely going today, for the next few
days, or even several months out, depending on your trader type (long-term position, short-term, swing,
day trader, scalper). It doesn't make sense not to at least reference the higher time frame dollar and higher
time frame currency pairs you intend to trade. Having that macro view is essential for longevity as a trader
and achieving high accuracy in directional premise.

section_title: UNIVERSAL CONCEPTS AND TIME ZONE TRANSLATION
content:
A lot of weight is placed on directional premise for new traders; I get it, I just wanted to know if it would go
up or down. While I initially only understood markets going up, these concepts are universal and apply to
every tradable asset class. Some things are sensitive to specific pairs like Cable (British Pound USD) and
Fiber (EUR USD), especially time of day. I'll refer to times in my local New York time zone; you'll need to
translate that for your global location.

section_title: ICT KILL ZONES AND TIME SYNCHRONIZATION
content:
For example, two o'clock in the morning and four o'clock in the morning New York time is the 'sweet spot'
ICT Kill Zone for London Open. It can start an hour before or last until 5 AM New York time. The easiest
way to synchronize is to set a world clock (desktop app, smartphone) to New York time. I track all major
session times to observe when certain moves occur. It s easier to sync with my New York time perspective
when learning my market understanding.

section_title: THE SEESAWS EFFECT EXPLAINED
content:
Let's reiterate Risk On and Risk Off. When Risk On is in the marketplace, you'll see the dollar falling. When
the dollar rallies, it's a Risk Off scenario. Imagine a children's seesaw: if the dollar goes up, foreign
currencies go down; if the dollar goes down, foreign currencies go up. It s very simple, but new traders often
overcomplicate it.

section_title: DOLLAR STRENGTH AND MARKET WEAKNESS (RISK OFF)
content:
The easiest way to understand it is: if the dollar is going up, everything else equities, foreign currencies,
commodities will be going the opposite direction. Risk On is when the dollar goes down (a 'flight from the
dollar' into higher-yield seeking, more risky asset classes). The dollar is viewed as a safe haven (I say that
tongue-in-cheek). If the dollar is going up, that's a Risk Off scenario, equating to lower stock prices,
declining commodity prices, and declining foreign currencies. This vice-versa effect is why I study the dollar
first; it's the foundation for overall market direction.

section_title: TRUSTING MACRO TRENDS AND ASSET CLASS CORRELATIONS
content:
Is it right to the minute every time? No. But the benefit of higher time frame charts (monthly, weekly, daily)
is that macro trends exist for a long time, taking a large impact to stop or reverse. I'm teaching you to trust
the higher time frame premise, especially macro trends. These are derived from higher time frame charts
and asset class to index or dollar-based correlations. For a stock trader, the benchmark is the Dow 30, S&P
500, or Nasdaq 100. Similarly, look at the dollar but invert it.

section_title: TRADING AGAINST A RALLYING DOLLAR IS LOW PROBABILITY
content:
If the dollar is poised to rally, it's important to understand that trying to buy foreign currencies means you're
trading against an adverse market undertone. You'll be trading at the lowest probability for that move to
occur because a rallying dollar translates to lower foreign currency prices. Let's look at an example using
notes from a previous live session.

section_title: DOLLAR RESISTANCE AND SUPPORT
content:
I have notes on this dollar chart from a live session about a week ago, where I commented on the dollar's
expected direction and behavior. This level here was a resistance level price couldn't break above. I used
wicks and candle bodies as reference points. We broke through it midweek, then came back down the
previous week's Friday. I noted these levels: if price broke through and came back down, it would most
likely find support here or here. This line represents that if these two levels gave way, price would come
down to this level and expect support.

section_title: ANTICIPATING RISK OFF FROM DOLLAR SUPPORT
content:
When price comes down to this level, even if it sweeps below a previous Friday low, notice where it closes:
right at that same level. Then Sunday was an unchanged event, Monday tried to push above it, and
Tuesday rallied. Today, Wednesday, price has receded but traded higher. In this environment, where price
comes down to an expected support level, your mind should be thinking: we're in a move that's been going
higher, we broke resistance, and this may act as support.

section_title: DOLLAR RALLY (RISK OFF) IMPLIES FOREIGN CURRENCY SELL SCENARIOS
content:
So what does that translate to for Risk On / Risk Off? If we think price comes to this level and bounces
higher, that implies we're going to expect the US dollar to rally. This is a Risk Off scenario, because when
there's less risk-taking in speculation, the dollar will be bought there's a 'flight to quality.' This 'flight to
quality' sees firmer dollar prices and translates to lower moves or sell signals in foreign currencies.

section_title: TRANSLATING DOLLAR STRENGTH TO CABLE WEAKNESS
content:
Because I trade majors (British Pound USD, EUR USD, and occasionally Canadian or Aussie dollar), I
focus on Fiber and Cable. Understanding this relationship: when the dollar comes down to a support level,
that means from Friday, Sunday, Monday, and Tuesday, we're looking for sell scenarios to occur in Fiber
and Cable. If we expect the USD to rally, and the British Pound is the front currency in GBP/USD, then the
British Pound should decline.
X
- RAY VIEW:

section_title: LONDON OPEN KILL ZONE ON CABLE (15-MINUTE)
content:
Now we'll zoom in and look at the relationship between the two with an 'X-ray view' to judge if risk-on/risk
- off scenarios are truly underway. Switching to a fifteen-minute time frame for Cable. This down move in
here (previous week's Friday, Sunday, new week Monday) corresponds with the dollar's move down to
support, which began the 'hunting ground' for sell scenarios in Cable, Fiber, and other foreign currencies
against the dollar.

section_title: IDENTIFYING LONDON OPEN SELL SETUP IN CABLE
content:
This down move, this sell signal, shows a high, a low, and a rally up. When the scenario is poised for a
firmer dollar (risk off), we expect weaker prices in Cable (British Pound USD). In this environment, we apply
specifics like time of day. The high of this candle here, 8 GMT and 8:15 GMT, is what I teach as the ICT
London Open Kill Zone.

section_title: THE NEW TRADING DAY AND SUCKER PLAY
content:
My charts are in GMT, but 7 GMT (2 AM New York time) and 9 GMT (4 AM New York time) are typically
when the high or low forms during London session. This range consistently produces the daily high or low
multiple times a week. With this understanding of risk-on/risk-off, we can increase accuracy. I'll add a
period separator at 5 GMT, which is midnight New York time. That's what I call the new day.

section_title: ANTICIPATING THE JUDAS SWING
content:
This midnight New York time delineation is my beginning marker for the new day. If you're expecting the
dollar to be trading at a support level (Risk Off: dollar rallying, foreign currencies going lower), you'll expect
specific times of the day to translate into setups. You'll want to see a rally after midnight in New York. This
rally is the 'sucker play' that neophyte traders are lured into believing is a buying opportunity.

section_title: SYNCHRONIZING TIME FOR ANALYSIS AND LIVE SESSIONS
content:
This rally occurs around New York midnight (5 GMT). For you, the easiest way is to set a clock or world
clock to New York time. This ensures that when watching my videos or live sessions, you have the exact
times lined up with your charts, making sense of the price action. If you only do one or two London trading
sessions a week, this is all you need.

section_title: SELLING INTO THE RALLY
content:
When we're in an environment where Cable (British Pound USD) should decline because the dollar is
finding support, we expect price to rally up after midnight. We do not chase that, thinking it's going higher.
We are happy to see this. Emotions are permitted here because we expect this phenomenon to unfold. The
signal occurs as it starts to rally; looking at price moving in the opposite direction is optimal for entry.

section_title: OPTIMAL TRADE ENTRY (OTE) CONFIRMATION
content:
Price fell short of the seventy percent retracement level, just a pip below the actual seventy percent
retracement. But look at the level of rejection once it gets near that level boom, it takes off in the other
direction. By using the simple Fibonacci tool and the ICT Optimal Trade Entry (OTE), which looks for a
minimum of sixty two percent retracement up to seventy nine percent, ideally in the 'sweet spot' between
them, you find your entry.

section_title: SIMPLIFYING FIBONACCI AND TEACHING METHODOLOGY
content:
I do not use the classical seventy eight point six and sixty one point eight Fibonacci levels; I don't even
know what they are, nor do I care. I keep it simple (sixty two and seventy nine percent). While some argue
it's not as accurate, it works for me and is easier to teach. I speak to my children when teaching, aiming for
easily digestible content for them, ensuring they have these resources if something happens to me.

section_title: ANTICIPATING PRICE FOR CONFIDENCE BUILDING
content:
The rally up makes it optimal because there's a large degree for price not to go very far once this
framework is established. If the dollar is poised to trade higher, we want to see a 'sucker play' or rally to sell
into. This will seem scary for weeks and months until you desensitize yourself by drilling, studying, and
anticipating price not trading, not demo trading, but anticipating. This builds confidence and anticipatory
skills, which are essential.

section_title: IDENTIFYING REPEATING PATTERNS AND THE POWER OF THREE
content:
You cannot make money reacting to price; you must be anticipatory to identify the most likely phenomenon
and how it repeats. The simple notion of looking for a rally up after midnight is a repeating pattern. This is
important because I taught a principle I dubbed the 'Power of Three'.

section_title: OPEN, RALLY, DECLINE (GRAPHICAL DEPICTION)
content:
The 'Power of Three' is a simple idea for down days. If we look at a full daily range, typically price will open,
trade a very little bit above the opening price, then decline. What's the opening price? I reference two: 0
GMT and 5 GMT (New York midnight). How price trades above or below these levels is important. If the
dollar is rallying, I expect higher price rallies in foreign currencies after midnight.

section_title: ABOVE THE OPENING PRICE
content:
When we see that rally, we do not chase it; we are happy to see it because we're expecting it to unfold. We
are looking for the phenomenon to repeat to build confidence. Initially, price will open, and whatever it does
above the opening price (at midnight New York time), that's where the signal takes place. The sell signal
occurs above the opening price. Anything at or above the opening price is our 'selling kill ground'.

section_title: MASTERING THE UPPER PORTION OF THE DAILY RANGE
content:
Why is this important? My mentor Larry Williams wished he understood how people could sell in that upper
portion of the daily range and capture the big move lower. I took that as a challenge, spending twenty years
trying to master that very thing. I've figured out enough to make a 'wicked living.' The red portion of the box
on the chart represents where the sell signal occurs. Your idea is to be selling on an anticipated down day,
finding a sell signal here.

section_title: ANTICIPATING DAILY RANGE EXPANSION AND LONDON CLOSE LOW
content:
Once you're in a sell position, you want to anticipate the daily range expanding lower. It will go up and down
intraday, making lower lows, then retracing, then blowing through the new low again. Eventually, around
London close (10 AM to 12 PM New York time), the low of the day will form seventy percent of the time. It
can go as late as noon, or even 2 PM New York time (1800 GMT) on FOMC announcement days.

section_title: MARKET MAKERS AND PREDICTABLE DAILY RANGES
content:
These time parameters define your window. You're looking for the largest degree of time to permit the daily
range to unfold. The market makers are doing their work, capping the market high and low at specific times.
Volume really increases and enters at the highest point, then falls off in the latter portion of London close,
driving these internals.

section_title: OPEN RALLY DECLINE SCENARIO
content:
The market trades down, making a low. The big portion of this graphic depiction of a daily range shows the
opening, dilly-dallying around the opening price, then rallying up that's the 'Judas swing,' the 'open rally
decline' scenario. We're looking for the high of the day to be made first. I'm referencing New York midnight
as the beginning of my new trading day. I make a living doing this, so it's important you understand my
interpretation of price.
CHALLENGE:

section_title: TESTING THE PREDICTABILITY OF PRICE ACTION
content:
I challenge you to put it to the test. While not one hundred percent, this will give you what you're looking for.
It's what every trader seeks, but no one is willing to withdraw from their cherished views, indicators, and
techniques. If you set that aside and get to the core root of why price moves as it does, when it does, and
who's moving it, it becomes a much easier environment to work within.

section_title: CLASSICAL LONDON OPEN ICT SELL SCENARIO
content:
Opening into price above the movement above the opening price at midnight (New York) is the classical
London Open ICT sell scenario. We're expecting a bounce based on the dollar's support level (broken
resistance turned support), which was mapped out live in a session with witnesses. This anticipated
bounce means a risk off scenario. Going back to Cable, we're looking for sell scenarios, meaning a down
day and a move going lower.

section_title: OPEN RALLY DOWN CLOSE
content:
Daily charts will show this. On the Cable daily chart, you'll see the opening, then a small movement above
the opening price (referencing 0 GMT, but the rally from New York midnight is key). This 'open rally down
close' phenomenon is what I described graphically. Look at the prolonged down move in Cable; how many
days does it show this pattern? I gave a study to everyone to observe June's price action for these patterns.

section_title: ABUNDANT TRADING OPPORTUNITIES FROM CONSISTENT PATTERNS
content:
For the Cable's prolonged down move, let's count the days with an 'open rally in a down day close' (open,
high, then lower close). You'll find numerous instances. One, two, Monday: open, rally, down close. Open,
rally, lower close (intraday range below open). Open, rally, down close. Many, many times! If this generic
and simple information doesn't impress you, you haven't been paying attention. This answers directional
premise and trade opportunities.

section_title: INEVITABLE LOSSES AND THE GUARANTEE OF SUCCESS
content:
How many trading opportunities was that in just the last month? Tons! Even with the wrong bias, say trying
to sell every day, you might have two losses and two wins. It shouldn't bother you to have a losing day or be
wrong, because I, Michael J. Huddleston, guarantee you will lose money using my teachings. I'm telling you
upfront you're going to lose money.

section_title: MANAGEABLE LOSSES AND ADEQUATE GAINS
content:
If you listen to me, the losses you incur will be very manageable and won't take you out of the game. On
the same coin, when you listen and the trades pan out, the money available, if you follow all the rules, will
more than adequately erase the losing trades and show you a net gain. The amount of net gain depends
on your experience, trader level, and assumed risk.
ULTRA
- LOW RISK FOR NEW TRADERS:

section_title: ONE QUARTER OF ONE PERCENT
content:
Keeping risk limited is crucial, especially as a new trader or if you're new to my methods. You should be
trading with about one quarter of one percent risk, even in a demo account. I know it's not 'sexy,' but it's not
about making money right now; it's about understanding what you're doing. It's like learning auto
mechanics: you learn the theory before working on a Maserati.

section_title: IMPORTANCE OF THEORY AND AVOIDING PREMATURE LIVE TRADING
content:
Many of you who have followed me since 2010 should still be in theory because you rushed into live
trading. Then you email me saying you're not understanding, but a couple emails later you think you 'got it.'
No, you just happened to have the right move that day. You haven't been applying the tools to the plethora
of multiple setups that happen throughout the week. Focus on one trading pair to learn these concepts,
then apply to others if you wish.

section_title: INSTITUTIONAL SPONSORSHIP AND STOP LOSS RATIONALE
content:
If we're in an environment that is most likely going higher or lower for a long period, like this down move in
Cable, the dollar must have been rallying at the same time. This is how you get institutional sponsorship
behind your trades. If you see a Risk Off scenario (dollar rallying), you expect a decline in the British
Pound. You look for the 'open rally up,' sell into that, and expect a down move with a lower close (range
expansion). If you're afraid of being stopped out, throw that thought out now.

section_title: STANDARD 30-PIP STOP LOSS FOR LEARNING
content:
You need to understand time and price as it relates to stop loss placement. If we anticipate price trading up
to a certain point, understanding our range and being above the opening price, we want to sell. Where
should our stop be? Above this high, as textbooks teach. But I always talk about using a standard 30-pip
stop loss. You might think it's too big, but it would only be a big loss if your risk exposure was high.

section_title: PLACING THE 30-PIP STOP LOSS
content:
Let's say your entry point is at the sixty two percent Fibonacci line during London Open (around 3 AM New
York time). Taking that 30-pip range, you'd place your stop loss order 30 pips above your entry. For
example, entry at 1.6882, stop loss at 1.6912. This gives you that much space. What does it mean? If price
takes you out by trading to that level (or even 1.6909 factoring spread), that's a 30-pip loss.

section_title: THE POWER OF THE 30-PIP STOP LOSS STUDY
content:
Why do I use 30 pips? I want you to do a study: look at days that create down moves in London and make
the high. Take the highest high during 2 AM-4 AM New York time, add 30 pips to that, and count how many
days that 30 pips gets taken out. If the average daily range for a currency pair is one hundred pips, how
much of that is 30 pips? Roughly a third.

section_title: HIGH PROBABILITY OF THE 30-PIP STOP LOSS
content:
My idea behind this is unique. If this larger rectangle represents a one hundred-pip daily range, and we're
selling in the upper third of the range, what's the likelihood of it moving 30 pips past our entry and then
making a lower low and a lower close? It's unlikely unless it's economically driven by a major report or
market-moving event.

section_title: AVOIDING FOMC AND NON-FARM PAYROLL
content:
FOMC or non-farm payroll days are the two reasons why I do not trade. These events can easily blow
through 30 pips, clean out both sides of the market, and revert to the center of the range, making no money
for anyone unless they're an ultra-short-term scalper fading the move. FOMC is a joke; don't trade it. Non
- farm payroll offers no reason to be in it unless you're playing in a demo account.

section_title: CONSISTENT VOLATILITY AND RISK ADJUSTMENT
content:
You can do real trading for twenty or nineteen more days in the rest of the month, with days that are
absolutely in favor of volatility conducive to trading. That's why we like to use a 30-pip stop loss. Over time,
you will learn to trim that 30-pip stop loss. You can make a career leaving it at 30 pips.

section_title: POSITION SIZING AND LONG-TERM DEMO PRACTICE
content:
When you know your entry point and your 30-pip stop loss, your risk exposure should be no more than one
quarter of one percent. This ultra-low risk means you're not worrying about money. If you lose, it's so
minute it won't hurt you. You could take a hundred losses in a row with that and still not be out of the game.
I tell you to trade it in a demo account for six months because it takes that long to trust what you should be
doing.

section_title: THE IMPORTANCE OF TIME INVESTMENT AND TRUST
content:
If you're on a fast track and need to make life changes quicker than six months, do not send me emails
saying my stuff doesn't work because you couldn't quit your job in six weeks. It won't work like that. If you
do not invest six months of your hard-earned free time doing this, don't watch my material. My work
requires work. I've condensed two decades of insights and experience into what I believe is the best
material available, costing you nothing but your time.

section_title: PATIENCE, DESENSITIZATION, AND OVERCOMING FEAR
content:
It will take a lot of patience and will be 'expensive' in terms of time, because I have to force you to do this
over a long period. Otherwise, you won't glean what you need to know because you don't even know what
that is yet. It's about me taking you to the chart and looking at specific things repeatedly until you see it,
until you're desensitized to the fear of trading, losing money, and being wrong. Those are the two primary
fears.

section_title: EGO, FORUMS, AND LEARNING FROM EXPERIENCE
content:
We're not really afraid of missing moves, just disappointed. We're fearful of losing money and being wrong
the two primary pillars of fear in speculation. Other pillars include ego. Folks spending time in forums,
sharing insights to appear smart or better than others don't be in forums if you're a developing trader,
unless you're reading from someone with significantly more experience and worthwhile advice. You
shouldn't teach others if you're still developing, as you'll do more harm than good.

section_title: MY PERSONAL JOURNEY AND THE VALUE OF EXPERIENCE
content:
My greatest learning happened in the last eight years of my career. You guys want to quit your jobs in four
weeks, but I've been doing this for two decades, and my best understanding came later. I wish I knew this
stuff when I first started; it would have been a lot different, less painful, and self-inflicted. I hope you learn
from my real problems, even physical ones, that trading can inflict.

section_title: CONTROL OVER TRADING DECISIONS
content:
It'll make you think you're bigger or smarter than you are, and also much less of a person. These
marketplaces can have a lot of influence over us, but it's all manageable and controllable. You have control
over it. Nothing forces you to put on a trade with five percent risk. Nothing forces you to pass on a trade
except common sense. You're in the driver's seat.

section_title: SUCCESS STORIES AND HELPING OTHERS
content:
I don't want credit for your winning or losing trades. This is about what you do at the end of this
development. I want to see what you do with this information. Once you're successful, how will you help
other people? That's what I'm doing for free. I'm not asking for anything except for you to share your story
with me. There won't be any upsell workshops or 'hooky software programs' later.

section_title: REJECTING LAZINESS AND AUTOMATED SOLUTIONS
content:
You don't need all that stuff. If you're looking for software programs to find trades for you, you're lazy. Lazy
doesn't pay. I tried that, and it never pans out. You need to understand how markets operate. You'll find
trades immediately; you'll know when they're going to happen. I don't need software to show me a pattern
on a chart. I know time-wise, daily, and day of the week it will occur.

section_title: ANTICIPATING MARKET MOVEMENT IN ADVANCE
content:
I know what time it's going to occur, how the pattern will unfold, and where it's most likely going to go
directionally. No software program can tell you that in advance. I can tell you the days of the week, months
in advance, when those patterns will happen because they're that generic. Everything else is playing 'catch
- up ball' to what you're learning. This is how the markets operate.

section_title: THE UNBREAKABLE GUARANTEE OF SUCCESS
content:
I'm telling you to challenge me on it. Go through the processes I'm sharing, and you tell me it doesn't
consistently work. I've already put it out there: I will give you ten thousand US currency cash to a PayPal
account if you show me how this does not consistently work and you can't be consistently profitable by
doing what I'm showing you. I guarantee you it's impossible for you to not be successful with this.

section_title: FRAMEWORK FOR INSTITUTIONAL SPONSORSHIP
content:
That's a very heavy statement, but I mean everything I'm telling you. If you do everything I'm showing you
development exercises, breaking the market down, having a risk-on/risk-off scenario, trading with
institutional sponsorship behind your ideas you'll have the framework that's cognizant of what the market
should be doing. If a bear market is underway, your daily charts will show down days. How do down days
typically start? With an up move intraday.

section_title: MY RANTS AND THE PRICE OF KNOWLEDGE
content:
That up move usually catches many traders by surprise ('Wait a minute, I bought that, and here I am
buying a high today!'). I did that, and you're probably doing it now. There's an answer to it, by taking
yourself out of textbooks and away from those selling you things. If they're selling that stuff, they need the
money; they aren't that good of a trader. I don't need your money or your thank-yous. I covet it because I
enjoy reading success stories, like 'Market Wizards.' I want more of that, and it's going to come through you
doing these very things. I know I'm on a soapbox, but that's part of being in these videos; you knew there
would be heavy trails, rants, and things you have to suck up.

section_title: LONDON SESSION SELLING AND PROFIT TARGETS
content:
Using this example, we understand we're in an environment where price should show weaker prices on
Cable while the dollar is poised to trade higher. In hindsight, that's a fact, but I had witnesses in a live
session where I mapped out these scenarios. If price comes down to support, it should go up. If that
happens in the dollar, we'll see weaker prices in foreign currency markets, translating to lower prices in
Cable and Fiber. We identified the selling scenario during London; once in position, how long do we hold?

section_title: TIME AND PRICE FOR PROFIT TAKING
content:
There are several things we like to do for profit taking: range projections using Fibonacci and swing
projections, and Average Daily Range (ADR). The ADR is an indicator I'll share. You can use Average True
Range (ATR) over five days; subtract that range from the highest high of the day to approximate where the
low should form. I use my own adopted ICT Average Daily Range indicator, and I also look at four-week,
weekly, and five-day average ranges.

section_title: PROFIT TAKING
content:
- LONDON CLOSE AND FIBONACCI EXTENSIONS
For a selling scenario, how long do we hold the trade? Time-wise, we look for 10 AM New York time (the
beginning of the London closed session). This is a general theory to test. While it might not give the exact
low of the day, you have two times of day where you're looking for the opposite end extreme of the day. In a
down day, with a high formed in London, the low is formed in New York and price starts to rally up (a
reversal market profile).

section_title: TAKING PROFITS AT KEY FIBONACCI EXTENSION LEVELS
content:
If you were to take profits from entering high and closing during the London close timeframe, would you be
upset if you didn't capture the absolute lowest low? If you wait for perfection, you'll miss opportunities for
better prices. So, how do we answer that? Time and Price. We incorporate a few other things. The 'Judas
swing' up, then the primary swing from low to the highest high. Once that low is taken out, we look for 127
percent, 162 percent, and 200 percent extensions. If price gets to any of these three levels during the New
York or London session, you take profits.
